news_summary: Significant progress in human rights includes enhanced abortion access in the EU, a major UN humanitarian system reform, strengthened child protection laws in the UK, and improved legal opportunities for rights in Egypt.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les progrès significatifs réalisés en matière de droits humains, on peut citer l'amélioration de l'accès à l'avortement dans l'UE, une réforme majeure du système humanitaire des Nations unies, le renforcement des lois sur la protection des enfants au Royaume-Uni et l'amélioration des possibilités juridiques en matière de droits en Égypte.
news_summary_es: Entre los avances significativos en materia de derechos humanos se incluyen la mejora del acceso al aborto en la UE, una importante reforma del sistema humanitario de las Naciones Unidas, el refuerzo de las leyes de protección infantil en el Reino Unido y la mejora de las oportunidades legales para el ejercicio de los derechos en Egipto.

articles.title: Landmark Win on Abortion in the EU
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Activists and representatives of the European Citizens’ Initiative "My Voice, My Choice" hold a press conference at Press Club Brussels Europe, presenting their campaign for safe and accessible abortion across the EU, after meeting with the European Commission, Brussels, Belgium, October, 1, 2025.
                    © 2025 Wiktor Dabkowski/ZUMA Press Wire/Shutterstock
          
    


  
On Thursday, the European Union Commission announced that the European Social Fund can be used by member states to fund safe abortions and improve access to abortion. This is in response to the European Citizens’ Initiative—My Voice, My Choice—which requested that “the EU does what is in its power to ensure safe and accessible abortion for all.”The My Voice, My Choice campaign garnered over 1 million signatures from EU citizens and was also supported by civil society organizations from all 27 member states. They argued that abortions were neither accessible nor affordable for many across the EU, especially marginalized women, and that this put women at risk of physical harm, undue stress, and financial strain. While the European Commission has not committed to introducing new legislation to reinforce this step, Hadja Lahbib, commissioner for equality, stated that Thursday’s decision means “support for women who need to travel; support for women in their own country; support for women in remote areas; support for women without financial means.” And that, in practice, “it means women will have better access to safe abortion care. Any vulnerable woman. Anywhere in Europe.” In the EU, only two countries—Poland and Malta—maintain highly restrictive abortion laws, permitting the procedure only in exceptional circumstances. Malta amended its legislation in 2023, easing what had previously been a total ban, while Poland further tightened access following a 2020 constitutional tribunal ruling. Although most other EU member states allow abortion on broader grounds, many permit broad conscientious objection by medical practitioners, which can significantly limit availability and delay care. International human rights bodies have repeatedly found that barriers to accessing abortion can violate rights to health, privacy, humane treatment, and nondiscrimination. But now women in countries with abortion bans or limited access could seek help abroad.This is a significant and long-overdue victory for women across the EU. It signals a renewed commitment to protecting reproductive rights and expanding access to safe abortion care. However, because health policy rests at the national level, now the responsibility lies with member states to ensure these funds translate into meaningful, tangible improvements on the ground. Governments across the EU should seize Thursday’s decision as an opportunity to strengthen access to abortion and uphold women’s rights.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Activists and representatives of the European Citizens’ Initiative "My Voice, My Choice" hold a press conference at Press Club Brussels Europe, presenting their campaign for safe and accessible abortion across the EU, after meeting with the European Commission, Brussels, Belgium, October, 1, 2025. © 2025 Wiktor Dabkowski/ZUMA Press Wire/Shutterstock

On Thursday, the European Union Commission announced that the European Social Fund can be used by member states to fund safe abortions and improve access to abortion. This is in response to the European Citizens’ Initiative—My Voice, My Choice—which requested that “the EU does what is in its power to ensure safe and accessible abortion for all.”

The My Voice, My Choice campaign garnered over 1 million signatures from EU citizens and was also supported by civil society organizations from all 27 member states. They argued that abortions were neither accessible nor affordable for many across the EU, especially marginalized women, and that this put women at risk of physical harm, undue stress, and financial strain.

While the European Commission has not committed to introducing new legislation to reinforce this step, Hadja Lahbib, commissioner for equality, stated that Thursday’s decision means “support for women who need to travel; support for women in their own country; support for women in remote areas; support for women without financial means.” And that, in practice, “it means women will have better access to safe abortion care. Any vulnerable woman. Anywhere in Europe.”

In the EU, only two countries—Poland and Malta—maintain highly restrictive abortion laws, permitting the procedure only in exceptional circumstances. Malta amended its legislation in 2023, easing what had previously been a total ban, while Poland further tightened access following a 2020 constitutional tribunal ruling. Although most other EU member states allow abortion on broader grounds, many permit broad conscientious objection by medical practitioners, which can significantly limit availability and delay care. International human rights bodies have repeatedly found that barriers to accessing abortion can violate rights to health, privacy, humane treatment, and nondiscrimination. But now women in countries with abortion bans or limited access could seek help abroad.

This is a significant and long-overdue victory for women across the EU. It signals a renewed commitment to protecting reproductive rights and expanding access to safe abortion care. However, because health policy rests at the national level, now the responsibility lies with member states to ensure these funds translate into meaningful, tangible improvements on the ground. Governments across the EU should seize Thursday’s decision as an opportunity to strengthen access to abortion and uphold women’s rights.
articles.reasoning: The EU Commission's decision to allow the European Social Fund to finance safe abortions represents a significant policy shift that improves access to reproductive healthcare for women across all member states. This move responds to a large-scale citizen initiative and addresses critical barriers faced by marginalized women, signaling a meaningful advancement in women's rights and health equity across Europe. The story has broad societal impact and clear substance, focusing on a concrete policy change with potential for tangible benefits.
articles.personality_title: EU allows funds to support safe abortion access across member states
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Access to safe abortion varies widely across the European Union (EU). Some countries have strict laws or limited services, making it hard for many women, especially those in remote or poor areas, to get the care they need.

**What happened** – On October 1, 2025, the European Commission announced that member states can now use money from the European Social Fund to help pay for safe abortions and improve access. This came after a campaign called “My Voice, My Choice” gathered over 1 million signatures asking the EU to support abortion rights.

**Impact** – This decision means that women who cannot get abortions in their own country, or who cannot afford them, may receive financial help to access safe care. It supports women who need to travel, live in remote places, or have little money. While this does not create new laws, it is a practical step to improve access across the EU, especially in countries with strict rules like Poland and Malta.

**What’s next step** – Now, each EU country must decide how to use this funding to make abortion care easier to get. The European Commission encourages governments to use this chance to improve women’s health services and protect their rights.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU’s new decision to fund safe abortion care marks a key step toward better access for women across Europe, especially those facing the greatest barriers.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’UE autorise le financement de l’accès à l’avortement sécurisé dans tous les États membres
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’accès à l’avortement sécurisé varie beaucoup dans l’Union européenne (UE). Certains pays ont des lois strictes ou peu de services, ce qui rend difficile pour de nombreuses femmes, notamment dans les zones isolées ou pauvres, d’obtenir les soins nécessaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 1er octobre 2025, la Commission européenne a annoncé que les États membres peuvent désormais utiliser des fonds du Fonds social européen pour financer des avortements sûrs et améliorer l’accès. Cette décision fait suite à la campagne « My Voice, My Choice » qui a recueilli plus d’un million de signatures demandant à l’UE de soutenir les droits à l’avortement.

**Impact** – Cette décision signifie que les femmes qui ne peuvent pas accéder à l’avortement dans leur pays ou qui n’ont pas les moyens peuvent recevoir une aide financière pour obtenir des soins sécurisés. Elle soutient les femmes qui doivent voyager, vivent dans des zones isolées ou ont peu d’argent. Même si cela ne crée pas de nouvelles lois, c’est un pas concret pour améliorer l’accès dans l’UE, notamment dans des pays aux règles strictes comme la Pologne et Malte.

**Prochaine étape** – Maintenant, chaque pays de l’UE doit décider comment utiliser ces fonds pour faciliter l’accès à l’avortement. La Commission européenne encourage les gouvernements à saisir cette opportunité pour améliorer les services de santé des femmes et protéger leurs droits.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle décision de l’UE de financer l’accès à l’avortement sécurisé est une étape importante pour améliorer l’accès aux soins pour les femmes dans toute l’Europe, surtout celles qui rencontrent le plus d’obstacles.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE permite financiar el acceso seguro al aborto en todos los Estados miembros
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El acceso al aborto seguro varía mucho en la Unión Europea (UE). Algunos países tienen leyes estrictas o pocos servicios, lo que dificulta que muchas mujeres, especialmente en zonas alejadas o pobres, puedan recibir la atención que necesitan.

**Qué pasó** – El 1 de octubre de 2025, la Comisión Europea anunció que los países miembros pueden usar fondos del Fondo Social Europeo para pagar abortos seguros y mejorar el acceso. Esto siguió a la campaña “My Voice, My Choice” que reunió más de un millón de firmas pidiendo que la UE apoyara los derechos al aborto.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión significa que las mujeres que no pueden abortar en su país o que no tienen dinero pueden recibir ayuda para acceder a atención segura. Apoya a mujeres que deben viajar, viven en áreas remotas o tienen pocos recursos. Aunque no crea nuevas leyes, es un paso práctico para mejorar el acceso en la UE, especialmente en países con reglas estrictas como Polonia y Malta.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora, cada país de la UE debe decidir cómo usar estos fondos para facilitar el aborto. La Comisión Europea anima a los gobiernos a aprovechar esta oportunidad para mejorar los servicios de salud y proteger los derechos de las mujeres.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La nueva decisión de la UE para financiar el aborto seguro es un paso clave para mejorar el acceso para mujeres en toda Europa, especialmente para quienes enfrentan mayores dificultades.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse group of stylized, faceless female silhouettes standing united in a circle, each holding a glowing, protective orb symbolizing safe abortion access, set against a softly colored European map backdrop with gentle light rays connecting the silhouettes across different regions.

articles.title: UN unveils bold overhaul of strained humanitarian system
articles.summary: On Friday the General Assembly was briefed on the latest developments of the UN80 Initiative – a system-wide reform effort to make the organisation more effective and fit for the future – including progress on the New Humanitarian Compact and on training and research reforms.
articles.content:
Teams under the leadership of UN Secretary-General António Guterres have been working on a wide-ranging set of proposals, spanning peace operations, development, human rights and humanitarian action, as well as cross-cutting areas such as data, technology and shared services, since the Initiative was launched in March 2025.

This includes efforts to generate efficiencies within the UN Secretariat, strengthen the creation, implementation and review of mandates (decisions adopted by Member States that direct the work of the UN), and explore ways to strengthen coherence and collective delivery across the UN system.

The briefing on 27 February focused on proposals for streamlining humanitarian support and the potential merger of some of the UN’s training and research bodies.

UN Photo/Manuel Elías Guy Ryder, Under-Secretary-General for Policy, addresses the informal meeting of the General Assembly plenary to hear a briefing on the UN80 Initiative.

Guy Ryder, the UN Under-Secretary-General for Policy, began the Friday morning briefing with an overview of the progress of the UN80 Initiative over the past 12 months, and an acknowledgment that the complexity of the work has been challenging for Member States to stay on top of it.

He promised to deliver clear information and a full vision of the process in a forthcoming report.

Duplicated UN humanitarian efforts ‘no longer sustainable’

Tom Fletcher, the UN Emergency Relief Coordinator, led a briefing on the New Humanitarian Compact, a process of reforms to the organisation’s humanitarian operations, which are under exceptional strain as they attempt to address multiplying conflicts, intensifying disasters and pressure on international humanitarian law.

UN Photo/Manuel Elías Tom Fletcher (on screen, centre), Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator, addresses the informal meeting of the General Assembly plenary to hear a briefing on the UN80 Initiative.

Mr. Fletcher was joined by several leading officials from the UN system (Catherine Russell, Executive Director of the UN children’s fund; Amy Pope, Director-General of the UN migration agency; Barham Salih, the head of the UN refugee agency and Matthew Hollingworth, Assistant Executive Director of the World Food Programme). The officials all explained how their agencies are ramping up cooperation and, in the words of Mr. Fletcher, “working as one unit.”

Ms. Russell said that a system in which humanitarian agencies operate parallel warehouses, fleets, contracts, and logistics networks is no longer sustainable. “We are taking a real step towards meaningful coherence,” she declared. “When supply chains are integrated, children receive assistance faster. When nutrition systems are aligned, fewer children become malnourished. When diplomacy is coordinated, access improves.”

Mr. Fletcher, who lauded the efforts of the five UN aid bodies to work more closely together, noted that, at a time when humanitarian needs are rising faster than the available resources, the collaboration that is already taking place is making a tangible difference on the ground.

“It's absolutely vital that we reform,” said the humanitarian chief, “because we know the impact of what we do. The reset is about defining more clearly our work around life-saving priorities first, radical reform to the efficiency in the way we deliver, and defending our values and our principles, which are under sustained attack.”

Fixing a fragmented training and research system

Training and research are vital functions of the UN system, but the landscape is fragmented, with overlapping functions, high costs and financial vulnerabilities.

Professor Tshilidzi Marwala, the Rector of UN University (UNU) and Michelle Gyles-McDonnough, the Executive Director of the UN Institute for Training and Research (UNITAR) described reform proposals which would involve the merging of training and research bodies, and a UN System Coordination Mechanism aimed at a more efficient use of resources.

Mr. Marwala also shared ideas for an AI platform to help address disinformation and strengthen links between research and training. “In a complex organization such as the UN, expertise and operational experience are often dispersed across entities,” he said. “Such a platform could connect operational expertise and Member State needs with research priorities while translating research findings into practical training tools.”

© WFP/Musa Abema Newly displaced people line up for WFP asssitance in South Kivu Province, DR Congo.

Next steps towards a ‘fit for purpose’ UN

The next major update of the work of the UN80 Initative for civil society is scheduled to take place at a Town Hall in late March. A high-level event co-designed with civil society is also planned for the High-Level Political Forum (HLPF) in July.

The Secretary-General aims to be able to demonstrate to Heads of State and government by the September high-level week of the General Assembly (UNGA 81) that the UN system is effectively meeting its responsibilities, changing and improving with serious levels of ambition.

“What is at stake,” said Mr. Ryder, “is no less than whether the United Nations system can rise to this moment and deliver on its commitments to build a UN that is truly fit for purpose and capable of responding effectively to today's challenges and tomorrow's uncertainties.”

Progress under the UN80 Initiative can be tracked through a public dashboard, which provides an overview of actions, timelines and implementation across the system.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant and broad reform effort by the United Nations to overhaul and improve the global humanitarian system, making it more efficient, coherent, and capable of addressing rising humanitarian needs. This reform has meaningful positive impact on humanitarian aid delivery worldwide, benefiting large populations affected by crises. The article provides detailed context on the reform initiatives, cooperation among UN agencies, and plans for future improvements, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: UN launches major reforms to improve global humanitarian aid
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The United Nations (UN) has been facing growing challenges in delivering humanitarian aid due to increasing conflicts, disasters, and limited resources. To address these problems, the UN started the UN80 Initiative in March 2025 to make the organization more effective and better prepared for the future.

**What happened** – On February 27, 2026, the UN General Assembly received an update on the progress of the UN80 Initiative. This included plans to improve humanitarian support by making agencies work more closely together and reduce duplicated efforts. Leaders from several UN agencies explained how they are combining resources like warehouses and transportation to help people faster. The UN is also planning to merge some training and research units to save money and improve how staff learn and share knowledge. They even proposed using artificial intelligence to connect research with real-world needs.

**Impact** – These reforms are important because they help the UN deliver aid more quickly and efficiently to people in crisis. For example, when supply chains are combined, children get food and medicine faster, and when nutrition programs work together, fewer children become malnourished. The changes also protect the UN’s core values while saving costs and improving cooperation among agencies, which has been hard before because of overlapping jobs and separate systems.

**What’s next step** – The UN will share more updates with civil society groups in March and hold a major event in July to discuss progress. By September 2026, the Secretary-General hopes to show world leaders that the UN is improving and ready to meet today’s and tomorrow’s challenges. People can also follow progress online through a public dashboard.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN is making important changes to work better as one team, helping people in need faster and more efficiently around the world.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU lance de grandes réformes pour améliorer l’aide humanitaire mondiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Organisation des Nations Unies (ONU) fait face à des défis croissants pour fournir une aide humanitaire à cause de conflits, de catastrophes et de ressources limitées. Pour y remédier, l’ONU a lancé en mars 2025 l’Initiative UN80 afin de rendre l’organisation plus efficace et mieux préparée pour l’avenir.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 27 février 2026, l’Assemblée générale de l’ONU a reçu une mise à jour sur les progrès de l’Initiative UN80. Cela comprenait des plans pour améliorer le soutien humanitaire en rapprochant les agences et en réduisant les efforts doublonnés. Des responsables de plusieurs agences ont expliqué comment ils combinent des ressources comme des entrepôts et des transports pour aider plus vite. L’ONU prévoit aussi de fusionner certains centres de formation et de recherche pour économiser et mieux former le personnel. Ils ont même proposé d’utiliser l’intelligence artificielle pour relier la recherche aux besoins concrets.

**Impact** – Ces réformes sont importantes car elles permettent à l’ONU d’apporter l’aide plus rapidement et efficacement aux personnes en crise. Par exemple, quand les chaînes d’approvisionnement sont unifiées, les enfants reçoivent plus vite nourriture et médicaments, et quand les programmes de nutrition travaillent ensemble, moins d’enfants souffrent de malnutrition. Ces changements protègent aussi les valeurs de l’ONU tout en économisant et améliorant la coopération entre agences, ce qui était difficile auparavant à cause des fonctions qui se chevauchent.

**Prochaines étapes** – L’ONU partagera d’autres informations avec la société civile en mars et tiendra un grand événement en juillet pour discuter des progrès. En septembre 2026, le Secrétaire général espère montrer aux dirigeants mondiaux que l’ONU s’améliore et est prête à relever les défis d’aujourd’hui et de demain. Le public peut aussi suivre les progrès en ligne via un tableau de bord public.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’ONU met en place des changements importants pour mieux travailler en équipe et aider plus rapidement et efficacement les personnes dans le besoin dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU lanza grandes reformas para mejorar la ayuda humanitaria global
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Organización de las Naciones Unidas (ONU) enfrenta crecientes desafíos para entregar ayuda humanitaria debido a conflictos, desastres y recursos limitados. Para solucionar estos problemas, la ONU inició en marzo de 2025 la Iniciativa UN80 para hacer la organización más efectiva y preparada para el futuro.

**Qué pasó** – El 27 de febrero de 2026, la Asamblea General de la ONU recibió una actualización sobre el progreso de la Iniciativa UN80. Esto incluyó planes para mejorar el apoyo humanitario haciendo que las agencias trabajen más unidas y reduciendo esfuerzos duplicados. Líderes de varias agencias explicaron cómo están combinando recursos como almacenes y transporte para ayudar más rápido. La ONU también planea unir algunas unidades de capacitación e investigación para ahorrar dinero y mejorar el aprendizaje y el intercambio de conocimientos. Incluso propusieron usar inteligencia artificial para conectar la investigación con las necesidades reales.

**Impacto** – Estas reformas son importantes porque ayudan a la ONU a entregar ayuda más rápido y eficientemente a las personas en crisis. Por ejemplo, cuando las cadenas de suministro se combinan, los niños reciben alimentos y medicinas más rápido, y cuando los programas de nutrición trabajan juntos, menos niños sufren de desnutrición. Los cambios también protegen los valores centrales de la ONU mientras ahorran costos y mejoran la cooperación entre agencias, algo que antes era difícil por trabajos y sistemas separados.

**Próximo paso** – La ONU compartirá más actualizaciones con grupos de la sociedad civil en marzo y realizará un evento importante en julio para discutir los avances. Para septiembre de 2026, el Secretario General espera mostrar a los líderes mundiales que la ONU está mejorando y lista para enfrentar los retos de hoy y del futuro. La gente también puede seguir el progreso en línea a través de un panel público.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La ONU está haciendo cambios importantes para trabajar mejor como un equipo, ayudando a las personas necesitadas más rápido y de manera más eficiente en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious assembly of interconnected lanterns shaped like diverse humanitarian aid supplies—food crates, medical kits, water containers—glowing warmly as they float together over a calm, stylized globe, symbolizing unified and efficient global cooperation under the UN’s humanitarian reform.

articles.title: Boy assaulted by birth parents wins campaign for UK child cruelty register
articles.summary: Tony Hudgell, who lost both legs after his birth parents assaulted him, has fought to protect children.
articles.content: Thanking everyone who has supported the campaign, including Helen Grant MP, she added: "This journey has been emotional, challenging, and at times overwhelming, but it has never been just my fight."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant policy change in the UK to establish a child cruelty register, driven by the advocacy of a survivor of severe child abuse. This represents a meaningful advancement in child protection and human rights, with broad societal impact. The story focuses on a clear achievement and includes context about the campaign and its emotional significance.
articles.personality_title: Campaign by abuse survivor leads to UK child cruelty register
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Tony Hudgell was badly hurt by his birth parents when he was a baby. He lost both of his legs because of the serious injuries they caused. After his recovery, Tony wanted to help protect other children from similar harm.

**What happened** – Tony Hudgell worked hard to get a new law passed in the UK. This law created a child cruelty register, which will keep track of adults who have been found guilty of hurting children. The register aims to stop those people from working with or caring for children in the future. Many people, including MP Helen Grant, supported Tony’s campaign.

**Impact** – This new register is important because it helps keep children safer by making sure that adults who have hurt kids cannot easily get jobs where they might harm children again. It is one of the first times such a system has been created in the UK, showing progress in child protection.

**What’s next step** – The government will now set up the child cruelty register and decide how it will work day-to-day. Tony and supporters will likely continue to watch that the register is used properly to protect children.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tony Hudgell’s efforts turned his painful past into a new law that helps protect children across the UK from abuse.
articles.personality_title_fr: La campagne d'un survivant d'abus mène à un registre de la maltraitance infantile au Royaume-Uni
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Tony Hudgell a été gravement blessé par ses parents biologiques lorsqu'il était bébé. Il a perdu ses deux jambes à cause des blessures graves qu'ils lui ont infligées. Après sa guérison, Tony a voulu aider à protéger d'autres enfants de maltraitances similaires.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Tony Hudgell a travaillé dur pour faire adopter une nouvelle loi au Royaume-Uni. Cette loi a créé un registre de la maltraitance infantile, qui enregistrera les adultes reconnus coupables d'avoir maltraité des enfants. Ce registre vise à empêcher ces personnes de travailler avec ou de s'occuper d'enfants à l'avenir. Beaucoup de personnes, dont la députée Helen Grant, ont soutenu la campagne de Tony.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau registre est important car il aide à protéger les enfants en empêchant les adultes qui ont fait du mal aux enfants d'obtenir facilement des emplois où ils pourraient nuire à nouveau. C'est l'une des premières fois qu'un tel système est créé au Royaume-Uni, montrant un progrès dans la protection des enfants.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement va maintenant mettre en place le registre de la maltraitance infantile et décider comment il fonctionnera au quotidien. Tony et ses soutiens continueront probablement à veiller à ce que le registre soit bien utilisé pour protéger les enfants.

**En une phrase** – Les efforts de Tony Hudgell ont transformé son passé douloureux en une nouvelle loi qui aide à protéger les enfants à travers le Royaume-Uni contre les abus.
articles.personality_title_es: La campaña de un sobreviviente de abuso impulsa un registro de crueldad infantil en el Reino Unido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Tony Hudgell sufrió graves daños causados por sus padres biológicos cuando era bebé. Perdió ambas piernas debido a las graves heridas que le provocaron. Después de recuperarse, Tony quiso ayudar a proteger a otros niños de abusos similares.

**Qué pasó** – Tony Hudgell trabajó mucho para lograr que se aprobara una nueva ley en el Reino Unido. Esta ley creó un registro de crueldad infantil que guardará información sobre adultos que hayan sido declarados culpables de maltratar niños. El registro busca evitar que estas personas trabajen o cuiden de niños en el futuro. Muchas personas, incluyendo a la diputada Helen Grant, apoyaron la campaña de Tony.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo registro es importante porque ayuda a mantener a los niños más seguros al impedir que adultos que han lastimado a niños consigan fácilmente trabajos donde puedan hacer daño otra vez. Es una de las primeras veces que se crea un sistema así en el Reino Unido, mostrando un avance en la protección infantil.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno ahora establecerá el registro de crueldad infantil y decidirá cómo funcionará día a día. Tony y sus seguidores probablemente seguirán vigilando que el registro se use correctamente para proteger a los niños.

**Una frase clave** – Los esfuerzos de Tony Hudgell convirtieron su pasado doloroso en una nueva ley que ayuda a proteger a los niños en todo el Reino Unido contra el abuso.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a strong, protective shield made of intertwined hands gently cradling a small, glowing silhouette of a child, surrounded by soft, natural tones symbolizing care and resilience, set against a calm, abstract background that conveys hope and community support.

articles.title: A Small Change in Egypt’s Tax Code, A Big Opportunity for Rights
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              People walk past a campaign poster of Egypt's President Abdel Fattah al-Sisi in a market in Cairo ahead of the presidential election, December 7, 2023.
                    © 2023 Khaled Desouki/AFP via Getty Images
          
    


  
In an overdue but welcome step, Egypt’s Ministry of Finance has published data showing state owned enterprises, following 2024 tax reforms to remove their tax exemptions, contributed 67 billion pounds (US$1.4 billion) in tax revenue in FY2024/25 and is expected to raise 87 billion pounds (US$1.7 billion) in FY2025/26. While Human Rights Watch cannot verify the government data, it shows that most of this revenue came from a small number of military businesses such as cement factories and military social and sporting clubs. Since Egyptian President Abdel Fattah al-Sisi seized power in 2014, he has greatly expanded the reach of the military’s businesses into a massive swath of the civilian economy. These opaque businesses operate in virtually all sectors from poultry to gas stations. For decades, they have largely not been subject to civilian oversight, transparency requirements, or taxation, so their revenues have not contributed to the government’s budget or fulfilling socioeconomic and other rights. Instead, they provide the military a revenue stream outside the state budget and have helped President Sisi and military generals solidify control over civilian life through authoritarian repression. Military businesses have also been linked to abusiveconduct with impunity.  For years, HumanRightsWatch and other groups have advocated for transparency around Egypt’s military businesses. As part of the International Monetary Fund’s (IMF) ongoing program, the government committed to publish the cost of tax exemptions for state-owned enterprises. It also enacted legal amendments in 2023 and 2024 to remove such exemptions, albeit with overbroad defense and national security exceptions. Last week, the IMF approved the release of US$2.27 billion as part of the US$8 billion deal.The recent disclosures are unprecedented. Although they are far from detailed, they show that taxing even a small number of military businesses in Egypt generates billions in revenue. This confirms what democracy and human rights advocates have been pointing out for years: broad tax exemptions for state-owned enterprises like military business rob the state of significant revenue that could help fulfill Egyptians’ economic, social, and cultural and other rights. A government report published in April 2024 under the IMF program estimated that total revenue lost to tax exemptions, including for state owned enterprises amounted to between 3 and 4.5 percent of GDP. By contrast, the country’s 2025/26 education budget was only 1.5 percent of GDP. Human Rights Watch has previously found that the Egyptian government has severely undermined the rights to education and health care by failing to allocate sufficient spending.That a seemingly obscure tax reform could generate such huge sums highlights the transformative potential of tax policy for human rights. But to unlock that potential, the revenue should support increased spending on rights, such as concrete outcomes on health, education, and social security.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image People walk past a campaign poster of Egypt's President Abdel Fattah al-Sisi in a market in Cairo ahead of the presidential election, December 7, 2023. © 2023 Khaled Desouki/AFP via Getty Images

In an overdue but welcome step, Egypt’s Ministry of Finance has published data showing state owned enterprises, following 2024 tax reforms to remove their tax exemptions, contributed 67 billion pounds (US$1.4 billion) in tax revenue in FY2024/25 and is expected to raise 87 billion pounds (US$1.7 billion) in FY2025/26.

While Human Rights Watch cannot verify the government data, it shows that most of this revenue came from a small number of military businesses such as cement factories and military social and sporting clubs.

Since Egyptian President Abdel Fattah al-Sisi seized power in 2014, he has greatly expanded the reach of the military’s businesses into a massive swath of the civilian economy. These opaque businesses operate in virtually all sectors from poultry to gas stations. For decades, they have largely not been subject to civilian oversight, transparency requirements, or taxation, so their revenues have not contributed to the government’s budget or fulfilling socioeconomic and other rights. Instead, they provide the military a revenue stream outside the state budget and have helped President Sisi and military generals solidify control over civilian life through authoritarian repression. Military businesses have also been linked to abusiveconduct with impunity.

For years, HumanRightsWatch and other groups have advocated for transparency around Egypt’s military businesses. As part of the International Monetary Fund’s (IMF) ongoing program, the government committed to publish the cost of tax exemptions for state-owned enterprises. It also enacted legal amendments in 2023 and 2024 to remove such exemptions, albeit with overbroad defense and national security exceptions. Last week, the IMF approved the release of US$2.27 billion as part of the US$8 billion deal.

The recent disclosures are unprecedented. Although they are far from detailed, they show that taxing even a small number of military businesses in Egypt generates billions in revenue. This confirms what democracy and human rights advocates have been pointing out for years: broad tax exemptions for state-owned enterprises like military business rob the state of significant revenue that could help fulfill Egyptians’ economic, social, and cultural and other rights.

A government report published in April 2024 under the IMF program estimated that total revenue lost to tax exemptions, including for state owned enterprises amounted to between 3 and 4.5 percent of GDP. By contrast, the country’s 2025/26 education budget was only 1.5 percent of GDP. Human Rights Watch has previously found that the Egyptian government has severely undermined the rights to education and health care by failing to allocate sufficient spending.

That a seemingly obscure tax reform could generate such huge sums highlights the transformative potential of tax policy for human rights. But to unlock that potential, the revenue should support increased spending on rights, such as concrete outcomes on health, education, and social security.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant policy reform in Egypt removing tax exemptions for military-owned state enterprises, generating substantial new government revenue. This has broad societal implications as it can potentially fund improved social services like education and healthcare, advancing economic and social rights. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful step toward transparency and resource allocation that could benefit many Egyptians.
articles.personality_title: Egypt’s new tax law brings billions from military businesses into public budget
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, Egypt’s military-owned businesses operated without paying taxes. These businesses run many companies in different industries but kept their earnings hidden from public budgets. This meant the government missed out on money that could help pay for schools, hospitals, and other services.

**What happened** – In 2024, Egypt changed its tax laws to remove tax exemptions for some state-owned companies, including military businesses. As a result, these businesses paid 67 billion Egyptian pounds (about US$1.4 billion) in taxes in the 2024/25 financial year. The government expects this number to grow to 87 billion pounds (US$1.7 billion) in 2025/26. This is the first time the government shared this information openly.

**Impact** – This change is important because it shows that taxing military businesses can bring in a lot of money. Before, these earnings were hidden and did not help improve public services. Now, the extra tax money could be used to support education, healthcare, and social security, areas where Egypt has struggled to spend enough.

**What’s next step** – The government and international groups like the International Monetary Fund (IMF) will watch how the new tax revenues are used. The hope is that this money will go to programs that improve people’s daily lives. More transparency and detailed reports about these revenues will also be important to keep track of progress.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By removing tax breaks for military businesses, Egypt is unlocking billions in new public funds that could improve education, health, and social services for its people.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle loi fiscale en Égypte rapporte des milliards des entreprises militaires au budget public
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant de nombreuses années, les entreprises militaires en Égypte ne payaient pas d’impôts. Ces entreprises dirigent de nombreuses sociétés dans différents secteurs, mais leurs revenus étaient cachés au budget public. Cela signifiait que le gouvernement manquait d’argent pour financer écoles, hôpitaux et autres services.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, l’Égypte a modifié ses lois fiscales pour supprimer les exemptions d’impôts pour certaines entreprises publiques, y compris celles militaires. En conséquence, ces entreprises ont payé 67 milliards de livres égyptiennes (environ 1,4 milliard de dollars US) d’impôts en 2024/25. Le gouvernement prévoit que ce chiffre atteindra 87 milliards de livres (1,7 milliard de dollars US) en 2025/26. C’est la première fois que ces informations sont publiées.

**Impact** – Ce changement est important car il montre que taxer les entreprises militaires peut rapporter beaucoup d’argent. Avant, ces revenus étaient cachés et ne servaient pas à améliorer les services publics. Maintenant, cet argent supplémentaire pourrait aider à financer l’éducation, la santé et la sécurité sociale, domaines où l’Égypte dépense trop peu.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouvernement et des groupes internationaux comme le Fonds monétaire international (FMI) surveilleront l’utilisation de ces nouvelles recettes fiscales. L’espoir est que cet argent aille à des programmes qui améliorent la vie quotidienne des gens. Plus de transparence et de rapports détaillés seront aussi importants pour suivre les progrès.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En supprimant les exemptions fiscales pour les entreprises militaires, l’Égypte débloque des milliards de fonds publics qui pourraient améliorer l’éducation, la santé et les services sociaux pour sa population.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva ley fiscal en Egipto aporta miles de millones de empresas militares al presupuesto público
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, las empresas militares en Egipto no pagaban impuestos. Estas empresas manejan muchas compañías en diferentes sectores, pero sus ingresos estaban ocultos al presupuesto público. Esto significaba que el gobierno perdía dinero que podría usarse para escuelas, hospitales y otros servicios.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, Egipto cambió sus leyes fiscales para eliminar las exenciones de impuestos para algunas empresas estatales, incluyendo las militares. Como resultado, estas empresas pagaron 67 mil millones de libras egipcias (unos 1.4 mil millones de dólares) en impuestos en el año fiscal 2024/25. El gobierno espera que esta cifra aumente a 87 mil millones de libras (1.7 mil millones de dólares) en 2025/26. Esta es la primera vez que el gobierno comparte esta información públicamente.

**Impacto** – Este cambio es importante porque muestra que gravar a las empresas militares puede generar mucho dinero. Antes, estos ingresos estaban ocultos y no ayudaban a mejorar los servicios públicos. Ahora, este dinero extra podría usarse para apoyar la educación, la salud y la seguridad social, áreas donde Egipto ha tenido dificultades para gastar lo suficiente.

**Próximo paso** – El gobierno y grupos internacionales como el Fondo Monetario Internacional (FMI) vigilarán cómo se usan los nuevos ingresos fiscales. La esperanza es que este dinero se destine a programas que mejoren la vida diaria de las personas. También será importante tener más transparencia e informes detallados para seguir el progreso.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Al eliminar las exenciones fiscales para las empresas militares, Egipto está desbloqueando miles de millones en fondos públicos que podrían mejorar la educación, la salud y los servicios sociales para su gente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a golden scale balancing a solid, stylized military factory on one side and a glowing, open book and a vibrant school building on the other, set against a soft, natural-toned Cairo cityscape at dawn symbolizing the transformative shift from military economic control to social investment.
